posts.title: Rough Overview of SLAM
posts.text: This post is aimed for introducing basic SLAM knowledge, especially visual SLAM. Only the general pipeline will be list here, I won’t touch lots of algorithm details. This will be suitable for those who want to get rough overview of the SLAM. This post hasn’t been finished!!! In the future, I will write more posts about the SLAM components. What is SLAM Simultaneous localization and mapping(SLAM) consists in the concurrent construction of a model of the environment(the map), and the estimation of the state of the robot moving within it. – Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age To be easy, SLAM is a system which does Mapping(building a map) and Localization(locating the sensor) at the same time. Why we need SLAM First of all, we observe that the SLAM research done over the last decade has itself produced the visual-inertial odometry algorithm that currently represent the state-of-the-art. More generally, SLAM has directly led to the study of sensor fusion under more challenging setups (i.e., no GPS, low quality sensors) than previously considered in other literature (e.g., inertial navigation in aerospace engineering). The second answer regards the true topology of the environment. The third answer is that SLAM is needed for many applications that, either implicitly or explicitly, do require a globally consistent map. – Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age Types of SLAM CameraFor visual SLAM, there are three popular types of camera: Monocular Camera, Stereo Camera, RGB-D Camera. Although nowadays new types of camera have shown up, they havn’t been widely used. Monocular: Since monocular cameras are really cheap and easy to get, researchers pay lots of attention on the monocular SLAM. The main problem of Monocular SLAM is scale ambiguity. Stereo Camera: Use two cameras to recover the image depth. The Baseline(distance between two cameras) need to be known. When the baseline distance is larger, the stereo can see further. The main problem of stereo camera is difficult to calibration and high computation cost. RGB-D Camera: This camera type use structured light or ToF(Time-of-Flight) to get the depth map.Different from the stereo camera, RGB-D cameras don’t need to do lots of depth computation. The main problem of RGB-D camera is noisy data, limited field of view … RGB-D cameras are often used in indoor SLAM systems. For in-the-wild SLAM, it’s much more difficult to use RGB-D. Typical SLAM Pipeline The architecture of a SLAM system includes two main components: the front end and the back end. The front end abstracts sensor data into models that are amenable for estimation, while the back end performs inference on the abstracted data produced by the front end. – Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age The figure from paper shows the main components of SLAM pipeline. Though clear enough, it doesn’t explain enough details. Actually, I found people prefer to use following components to describe typical SLAM pipeline: Sensor Data Reading. Front End: Visual Odometry. Calculate camera motion between frames, build local map. Back End: Optimization. Accept camera pose of visual odometer, infomation of loop closing, then start optimization, to get global trajectory and map. Loop Closure. Detect loop. If exist, pass information to back end. Mapping. Build map. Visual OdometryVisual odeometry is the front-end of a SLAM system. It use adjacent images to estimate rough camera motion, and provide data for back-end. Basicly, there are two kinds of methods. Feature Based Methods. Feature based methods need to extract features from images first, and use those features to do computations. Features based methods are always viewed as the mainstream of visual odometry, because they are stable, and insensitive to light and motion. Direct Methods. Though feature is useful and robust, those methods suffer from the time consuming and feature losing. Direct methods could use all information in the images. Nowadays, direct methods are becoming important role in the SLAM area. Let’s have a close look at the feature based methods. Feature Extraction. Feature = key points + descriptor For SLAM, the feature should be robust, accurate and fast. ORB features are commonly used features. ORB features have scale and rotation invariance, and really fast. Epipolar Constraint. Epipolar constraint is basic geometry relationship in SLAM, and can be used to calculate camera motion. Triangulation. After using epipolar constrain t o get camera pose, we can use triangulation to fetch 3d points location. PnP Problem. When we already get 3D points locations, and we get 2D points, we can solve PnP problem to get camera locations. Point Cloud Matching: ICP. Back EndVisual odometry estimate short-term motion and structure. However, with the error accumulation, the map will gradully become unreliable. So, SLAM need to solve a long-term optimization problem to build large scale map. Extended Kalman Filter, EKF. With markov assumption, filter based methods like EKF use former state to predict next state. Bundle Adjustment. Current state is related to all the states before. Nonlinear optimization based methods like BA optimize all the states. Pose Graph. BA require high computaion. Pose graph give up landmard optimization, only optimize camera trajectory. Factor Graph. Loop ClosureLoop Closure add constraint for similar images, which helps to reduce error accumulation. The key is how to detect the loop. Bag-of-Words, BoW. BoW use word/dictionary to describe images. Using those words, we can calculate similarity between images. Similarity Calculation. MappingReference Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: Toward the Robust-Perception Age

posts.title: Hello World
posts.text: Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub. Quick StartCreate a new post1$ hexo new "My New Post" More info: Writing Run server1$ hexo server More info: Server Generate static files1$ hexo generate More info: Generating Deploy to remote sites1$ hexo deploy More info: Deployment

posts.title: Structure From Motion
posts.text: The structure from motion problem in computer vision is the problem of recovering the 3D structure of a stationary scene from a set of projective measurements, represented as a collection of 2D images, via estimation of the motion of the camera corresponding to these images. Before Reading This post doesn’t contain any code. So if you want to understand SfM better, I highly recommend you to run SfM by yourself. Colmap is a good start point. Some are copied from structure from motion revisit. Please have a look at the original paper. BackgroundKnowing the 3D structure of real world is meaningful. It’s a generally scientific problem and core technology of a wide variety of fields, such as computer graphics, computer vision, virtual reality, digital media, etc. The structure from motion is one of the most important algorithms to recover 3D structure. It provides a useful process of reconstructing 3D structure from its projections into a series of images taken from different viewpoints. Problem Formulation Input: A collection of 2D images. output: 3D point cloud Assumptions: The scene is static ; … OverviewFor general SfM, it involves the three main stages: Extraction of features in images and matching these features between images. Camera motion estimation. Recovery of the 3D structure using the estimated motion and features. A variety of SfM strategies have been proposed including incremental, hierarchical and glbal approaches. Arguably, incremental SfM is the most popular strategy for reconstruction of unordered photo collections. Incremental SfM commonly starts with feature extraction and matching, followed by geometric verification. The resulting scene graph serves as the foundation for the reconstruction stage, which seeds the model with a carefully selected two-view reconstruction, before incrementally registering new images, triangulating scene points, filtering outliers, and refining the reconstruction using bundle adjustment (BA). Correspondence SearchFeature ExtractionWe wish to the features to be invariant under radiometric and geometric changes so that SfM can uniquely recognize them in multiple images. SIFT, its derivatives, and more recently learned features are the gold standard in terms of robustness. input: A set of images. output: $F_{i}={(x_{j},f_{j})|j=1…N}$ of local features at location $x_{j}\in R^{2}$ represented by an appearance descriptor $f_{j}$. MatchingSfM discovers images that see the same scene part by leveraging the features $F_{i}$ as an appearance description of the images. The naive approach tests every image pair for scene overlap; it searches for feature correspondences by finding the most similar feature in image $I_{a}$ for every feature in image $I_{b}$, using a similarity metric comparing the appearance $f_{j}$ of the features. However, this approach has computational complexity $O(N^{2})$ and is prohibitive for large image collections. A variety of approaches tackle the problem of scalable and efficient matching. input：$F_{i}={(x_{j},f_{j})|j=1…N}$ output: A set of potentially overlapping image pairs $C={I_{a},I_{b}|I_{a},I_{b}\in I, a < b }$ and their associated feature correspondences $M_{ab}\in F_{a}\times F_{b}$. Geometric VerificationSince matching is based solely on appearance, it is not guaranteed that corresponding features actually map to the same scene point. Therefore, SfM verifies the matches by trying to estimate a transformation that maps feature points between images using projective geometry. A homography H describes the transformation of a purely rotating or a moving camera capturing a planar scene. Epipolar geometry describes the relation for a moving camera through the essential matrix E (calibrated) or the fundamental matrix F (uncalibrated), and can be extended to three views using the trifocal tensor. If a valid transformation maps a sufficient number of features between the images, they are considered geometrically verified. input: A set of potentially overlapping image pairs $ C={I_{a},I_{b}|I_{a},I_{b}\in I, a < b }$and their associated feature correspondences $M_{ab}\in F_{a}\times F_{b}$. output: Geometrically verified image pairs $C={I_{a},I_{b}|I_{a},I_{b}\in I, a < b } $and their associated feature correspondences $M_{ab}\in F_{a}\times F_{b}$. The output of this stage is a so-called scene graph with images as nodes and verified pairs of images as edges. Incremental ReconstructionInitializationSfM initializes the model with a carefully selected two-view reconstruction. Choosing a suitable initial pair is critical, since the reconstruction may never recover from a bad initialization. Image RegistrationStarting from a metric reconstruction, new images can be registered to the current model by solving the Perspective-n-Point (PnP) problem using feature correspondences to triangulated points in already registered images. The PnP problem involves estimating the pose $P_{c}$ and, for uncalibrated cameras, its intrinsic parameters. The set $P$ is thus extended by the pose $P_{c}$ of the newly registered image. TriangulationA newly registered image must observe existing scene points. In addition, it may also increase scene coverage by extending the set of points X through triangulation. A new scene point $X_{k}$ can be triangulated and added to $X$ as soon as at least one more image, also covering the new scene part but from a different viewpoint, is registered. Triangulation is a crucial step in SfM, as it increases the stability of the existing model through redundancy and it enables registration of new images by providing additional 2D-3D correspondences. Bundle AdjustmentImage registration and triangulation are separate procedures, even though their products are highly correlated uncertainties in the camera pose propagate to triangulated points and vice versa, and additional triangulations may improve the initial camera pose through increased redundancy. Without further refinement, SfM usually drifts quickly to a non-recoverable state. BA is the joint non-linear refinement of camera parameters $P_{c}$ and point parameters $X_{k}$ that minimizes the reprojection error$$E=\Sigma_{j}\rho_{j}(||\pi(P_{c},X_{k})-x_{j}||^{2}_{2})$$ using a function $\pi$ that projects scene points to image space and a loss function $\rho_{j}$ to potentially down-weight outliers. Reference structure from motion revisit A survey of structure from motion
